note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 4-day history of burning sensation during urination localized to her genital area. She reports increased urinary frequency but no aggravating factors or treatments tried so far. The symptom severity is moderate and has remained consistent since onset. She has a history of renal disease and is allergic to penicillin. She is not pregnant. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute uncomplicated cystitis 
- Differential: 
 1. Urinary tract infection (including cystitis) 
 2. Urethritis (possibly infectious or non-infectious) 
 3. Bladder stones or irritation secondary to renal disease 
 4. Interstitial cystitis or other non-infectious causes of dysuria 
- Red flags considered: No fever, flank pain, hematuria, or systemic symptoms reported that would suggest pyelonephritis or complicated UTI.

P: 
- Disposition: Manage as outpatient with close follow-up if symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop. 
- Plan: Given the history and symptoms consistent with cystitis, initiate empiric treatment avoiding penicillin due to allergy. Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated. Consider nitrofurantoin or fosfomycin as first-line agents given allergy and renal disease status. Encourage increased fluid intake and symptom monitoring. Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever, flank pain, or worsening symptoms occur. 
- Safety net: Educate patient on signs of complicated infection including fever, chills, flank pain, nausea, vomiting, or worsening dysuria. Instruct to return promptly if these develop or if no improvement within 48-72 hours after starting treatment.

evidence.title: Burn Evaluation: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
ep into the skin 
Is larger than 3 inches across 
Looks leathery and dry 
Has patches of white, brown, or black 
Swells very fast 
Gets more painful over time, has pus, or causes a fever , which may be signs of an infection 
Also, get medical help for all burns that:
Are caused by electricity or chemicals 
Are on the face, hands, genitals, buttocks, over a joint, or go all the way around a part of the body, such as a leg or the chest 
Happen with inhalation (breathing in) of smoke, hot gasses, or chemicals 
Happen to a child under age 5 or an adult older than age 70 
References 
Agrawal A, Raibagkar SC, Vora HJ. Friction Burns: Epidemiology and Prevention. Ann Burns Fire Disasters [Internet]. 2008 Mar 31 [cited 2023 Mar 8]; 21(1): 3-6. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3188131 
American Burn Association [Internet]. Chicago (IL): American Burn Association; c2022

Conditions A to Z
Cystitis 
Cystitis is a urinary tract infection (UTI) that affects the bladder. It's common, particularly in women. It often gets better by itself, but may sometimes be treated with antibiotics. 
Some people get cystitis frequently and may need regular or long-term treatment.
Symptoms of cystitis 
Symptoms of cystitis include:
pain, burning or stinging when you pee needing to pee more often and urgently than usual pee that's dark, cloudy or strong smelling pain low down in your tummy Symptoms in young children may also include:
a high temperature – they feel hotter than usual if you touch their neck, back or tummy wetting themselves reduced appetite and being s
